title: Admission Workups: Core 50
description: A portable library assembled from the 50 modular admission workup files.

items.text: Ask about onset, tempo, severity, triggers, relieving factors, and change from baseline.
Ask about chest discomfort, palpitations, syncope, diaphoresis, cough, fever, and hemoptysis.
Ask about prior heart or lung disease, prior admissions, baseline exertional tolerance, and home oxygen use.
Ask about tobacco or vaping exposure, immobility, thromboembolic risk, and relevant family history.
Ask about recent medication changes, adherence, inhaler or diuretic use, and anticoagulant use when relevant.
Ask about baseline ambulation, sleep position, activity limitation, and new functional decline.
Ask about rest versus exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and positional change.
Ask about cough, sputum amount or color, wheeze, pleuritic pain, fever, and hemoptysis.
Ask about chest pressure, palpitations, edema, rapid weight change, presyncope, and syncope.
Ask about recent immobilization, travel, surgery, cancer, prior DVT or PE, and unilateral leg symptoms.
Assess general appearance, respiratory rate and pattern, heart rate and rhythm, blood pressure, temperature, and oxygenation.
Inspect work of breathing and auscultate all lung fields for air movement and focal or diffuse adventitious sounds.
Assess cardiac rate and rhythm, heart sounds, murmurs, and signs of impaired peripheral perfusion.
Assess jugular venous pressure when feasible, mucous membranes, edema, and other bedside signs of volume status.
Inspect extremities for asymmetry, edema, cyanosis, calf tenderness, and distal pulses.
Assess alertness, orientation, and focal neurologic change that could accompany hypoxemia or poor perfusion.
Inspect ability to speak, accessory-muscle use, posture, respiratory fatigue, and audible wheeze or stridor.
Auscultate for focal crackles, diffuse wheeze, diminished breath sounds, and asymmetry.
Assess jugular venous pressure when feasible, peripheral edema, and other signs of congestion.
Compare both legs for swelling, tenderness, erythema, and asymmetry; assess distal pulses.
